item.title: Long neglected Kotna–Rulna Road upgrade restores hope
item.description: For many years, the people living along the Kotna-Rulna Road in Dei District, Western Highlands Province, have been cut off from basic government services because of poor road access. Kotna Local Level Government is often referred to as the "back page" of Dei District and Western Highlands due to its remote location, bordering Jimi District in Jiwaka Province and parts of Madang Province.
item.content:encoded:
For many years, the people living along the Kotna-Rulna Road in Dei District, Western Highlands Province, have been cut off from basic government services because of poor road access.

Kotna Local Level Government is often referred to as the "back page" of Dei District and Western Highlands due to its remote location, bordering Jimi District in Jiwaka Province and parts of Madang Province.

More than 10,000 people living in six council wards along the Kotna-Rulna Road have struggled to transport their garden produce, reach health services, attend schools, and travel to Mt Hagen City.

Although previous district administrations attempted to improve the road, the communities continued to face hardship because the road remained in poor condition.

Residents of Lekhan village welcomed the arrival of heavy machineries on Sunday, July 19, when locally owned road construction company, Bindon Construction, mobilised its equipment to begin upgrading the long-neglected Kotna–Rulna Road.

The arrival of the machines brought emotional scenes, with many women, men, young and old celebrating the start of a project they had waited for over many years.

" I am from Rulna the back page of Dei Council. Sometimes when I want to bring my garden produce to the nearest market at Kotna Station, I struggled because there is no PMV services because the road is not good for vehicles to pass through to Rulna where I am. I am very thankful to Dei MP Desmond Kipa to send the machines now the upgrade this road. Now I can say I will be able to bring my garden produce to the market and sell it," a tearful Margaret Koim said.

Some were even moved to tears, saying they never thought they would see road construction equipment reach their community.

The road upgrade is part of the Dei District Development Authority's infrastructure program aimed at improving road access across the district.

Speaking to the people at Lekhan, Dei DDA Procurement Board Member Douglas Minimbi said road construction and other basic government services are being delivered at the same time throughout the district.

Mr Minimbi said the DDA, under the leadership of Dei MP Desmond Paul Kipa, is not carrying out projects one by one but is working on several important developments at the same time to make the best use of the limited time available before completing its planned programs.

" MP Kipa just got office as the MP this year yet work as already progressed in all parts of the district thus we don't have enough time left and so work is running concurrently in all the three LLGs of the district," Mr Minimbi said.

He appealed to communities to protect the workers and heavy machineries and to avoid making unnecessary demands that could delay the project.

Mr Minimbi also thanked Bindon Construction for bringing in a specialised rock-cracking machine, saying it is the only local company currently equipped with such machinery to break large rocks and boulders that could slow road construction.

"I would also like to sincerely thank Bindon Construction for bringing in the rock cracking machine for this road upgrade. Rulna roads are very challenging, and the machine will do the work for the right purpose. Bindon Construction is the only company that has this rock cracking machine, and for the company to share their equipment with the people of Dei is very much appreciated," Mr Minimbi said.

Ward members from all six council wards along the Kotna-Rulna Road thanked the Dei DDA for finally responding to the needs of their people.

They assured the contractor that the communities would provide full support and ensure the safety of both workers and equipment throughout the project.

"We will definitely look after this service that is coming. Because the work will continue day and night, we will make sure nothing happens to the machines and the workers until the project finishes," a ward member of the six wards along the Kotna-Rulna Road said.

Bindon Construction Managing Director said the Kotna-Rulna Road will be one of the company's most challenging road projects because of the difficult terrain.

He also called on local communities to work together to protect the machineries so work can continue without interruption.

The company says four more heavy machines will soon arrive at the construction site to speed up the project, with work expected to be completed before October this year.

Bindon Construction is also nearing completion of the Dupaim–Kinabuga Road upgrade in Dei District.

Once completed, the Kotna-Rulna Road will not only improve travel for thousands of people in Western Highlands but will also benefit nearby communities in Jiwaka Province.

For families who have long been isolated, the road promises easier access to markets, schools, health services and government support-turning what was once known as the district's "back page" into a community connected to opportunity.

item.title: Remembrance Day observed in Northern province
item.description: In Northern Province, relatives and community members gathered at Buna beach front for a small but significant dawn service to mark the 84th anniversary of the 1942 World War two battle. Ex-servicemen, school children, and Seventh Day Adventist youth led the service at the site where the bloody Buna battle was fought.
item.content:encoded:
In Northern Province, relatives and community members gathered at Buna beach front for a small but significant dawn service to mark the 84th anniversary of the 1942 World War two battle.

Ex-servicemen, school children, and Seventh Day Adventist youth led the service at the site where the bloody Buna battle was fought.

An Anglican Priest offered special prayers for those who served in 1942, their families, and the generations since.

Addressing the people of Buna and surrounding coastal villages, prominent public servant, Steven Awoda said “we must always remember those who fought for our freedom and independence.” He urged people to observe Remembrance Day with its true meaning.

The service concluded with wreath laying at the memorial on Buna Beach.

item.title: Remote Hela community welcomes MAF support
item.description: ​Speaking during a recent visit to the Kulie Yokana community in the Koroba-Lake Kopiago District, Pastor Toliman Siape of the Christian Apostolic Fellowship has thanked MAF for its ongoing operations in Hela province. ​The Missionary Aviation Fellowship [MAF] in Hela Province continues to bring relief and hope to the isolated population.
item.content:encoded:
​Speaking during a recent visit to the Kulie Yokana community in the Koroba-Lake Kopiago District, Pastor Toliman Siape of the Christian Apostolic Fellowship has thanked MAF for its ongoing operations in Hela province.

​The Missionary Aviation Fellowship [MAF] in Hela Province continues to bring relief and hope to the isolated population.

Pastor Toliman says that without the air service, thousands of people would face severe daily hardships and near-total isolation.

​"It is not only Kopiago-MAF plays an essential role across the country by airlifting essential goods, medical supplies, educational materials, and passenger travel to the outside world," Pastor Siape said.

"Without this service, our people would lead lives of immense struggle, completely cut off from basic necessities."

​Pastor Toliman noted that MAF adjusts its airfares to accommodate rural villagers who have limited access to economic opportunities and cash-generating activities.

​He also commended the Kulie Yokana community for taking ownership of their development by hand-building their local airstrip to make MAF flights possible.

​He urged the people of Hela Province to adopt a spirit of self-reliance rather than relying solely on state intervention, encouraging communities to initiate local projects to attract government support.

item.title: Daru celebrates Remembrance Day
item.description: Western Province marked Remembrance Day on Thursday with a solemn ceremony in Daru, honoring local men who served as carriers and guides during the Kokoda Campaign in World War Two. Provincial Administrator Robert Alphonse Kaiyun delivered the keynote address, remembering the sacrifices of Western Province men who supported the allied soldiers [Australia and United States] along the Kokoda track.
item.content:encoded:
Western Province marked Remembrance Day on Thursday with a solemn ceremony in Daru, honoring local men who served as carriers and guides during the Kokoda Campaign in World War Two.

Provincial Administrator Robert Alphonse Kaiyun delivered the keynote address, remembering the sacrifices of Western Province men who supported the allied soldiers [Australia and United States] along the Kokoda track.

"For us from Western Province, we stood together along the track. We guided our soldiers through the jungles and mountains. They were called the 'Fuzzy Wuzzy Angels'. But to us, we remember that they were our families," Mr Kaiyun said.

He highlighted the role of the province during the war.

"During the war, our waters, our waterfronts, and our people were part of the defence of our country. Today, Western Province is a province of resilience, of looking out for each other,"he said.

Speaking to students, families of the veterans, community leaders, public and the Western Provincial Administration staff who gathered for the event, Mr Kaiyun said remembrance must go beyond ceremonies.

"Remembrance is not just the past. It's about the values that they have left behind for us. Courage is when things are hard. Sacrifice for others. If we live these values today, in our schools, in our homes, in our workplaces, in how we serve our province and country ,then we honour them properly," he said. He closed with a direct call to the younger generation.

"We say names and we observe silence. But more than that, we must make a promise that we will remember them," Mr Kaiyun said.

Meantime, wreaths were laid. Heads bowed down and for a minute Daru stood still to remember. Lest We Forget.

item.title: South Fly women recovering after suspected river related illness
item.description: Two women from the Fly River area in the Fly Kiwai RLLG in the South Fly District of Western Province are recovering in Daru General Hospital after suffering severe skin reactions they believe were caused by contamination in the Fly River. Rose Sapsi, 20, from Gaima Village and Kehama Thomas, 27, from U'uwo Village in the Kiwai Islands were admitted last weekend after developing intense swelling,
item.content:encoded:
Two women from the Fly River area in the Fly Kiwai RLLG in the South Fly District of Western Province are recovering in Daru General Hospital after suffering severe skin reactions they believe were caused by contamination in the Fly River.

Rose Sapsi, 20, from Gaima Village and Kehama Thomas, 27, from U'uwo Village in the Kiwai Islands were admitted last weekend after developing intense swelling, itching and burning while making sago.

One woman was taken in from U'uwo Village and the other patient came in from Gaima Village along the Fly River area.

Both were referred from Teapopo Health Centre. Hospital staff confirm their condition is now improving and they are responding to treatment.

Speaking from her hospital bed, Sapsi described what happened.

"I went to the sago place at Abo last Thursday to make sago, that’s when this thing happened. While beating the sago I felt my body was itching and a burning feeling on my skin. It felt like fire was burning my skin and so I ran and jumped into the river but that did not help. My body started to swell in pain. My husband carried me to the canoe and paddled to the village then all the way to Teapopo Health Centre,"Sapsi said.

She said they were only treated with amoxicillin and Panadol but that did not help and so they were referred to Daru General Hospital.

Another patient, Kehama Thomas, also shared a similar encounter at U'uwo Village while she was beating sago.

The women told NBC News that many other women along the Fly River are experiencing similar symptoms. They say the reactions started after contact with river water at their sago camps.

They are calling on the Western Provincial Health Authority and Ok Tedi Mining Limited to urgently investigate and test water sources in affected areas to prevent more cases.

NBC News is seeking comments from Ok Tedi Mining Limited.

The Western Provincial Health Authority has quickly confirmed that they will send a team to investigate the matter at Gaima village on Friday.

At this stage, health authorities have not confirmed the cause of the illness. However, water and medical testing has been requested.

item.title: Team PNG ready for Commonwealth Games
item.description: Team PNG is ready to take its place on the international stage as the countdown ends for the opening of the Glasgow 2026 Commonwealth Games. The Opening Ceremony will be held at the OVO Hydro Arena on Thursday evening in Scotland, which is 5:00am Papua New Guinea time on Friday.
item.content:encoded:
Team PNG is ready to take its place on the international stage as the countdown ends for the opening of the Glasgow 2026 Commonwealth Games.

The Opening Ceremony will be held at the OVO Hydro Arena on Thursday evening in Scotland, which is 5:00am Papua New Guinea time on Friday.

Papua New Guinea will compete in boxing, weightlifting, women's 3x3 basketball, swimming and athletics.

Team PNG Chef de Mission Elaine Iorive said the athletes have worked hard to prepare for one of the biggest sporting events on the Commonwealth calendar.

"After months of training and preparation, Team PNG is proud and excited to be part of the Commonwealth Games Opening Ceremony. Marching behind our flag on the world stage is a special moment for every athlete and official," she said.

Ms Iorive said the team is ready to represent Papua New Guinea with pride and showcase the country's resilience, talent and sporting spirit.

"We are ready to represent Papua New Guinea with pride and to showcase the resilience, talent and spirit of our people throughout the Games."

Team PNG begins competitions on Friday in the women's 3x3 basketball, swimming and boxing.

The Glasgow 2026 Opening Ceremony will also make Commonwealth Games history as the first to be staged entirely inside an indoor arena.

item.title: PNG go down brave to New Zealand in OFC U16 semis
item.description: New Zealand have qualified for a 13th FIFA U-17 World Cup and secured progression to the final of OFC U-16 Men's Championship 2026 with a 3-0 win over Papua New Guinea at the PNG Football Stadium in Port Moresby. They move on to face Fiji in Saturday's title-decider at the same venue, overcoming a spirited display from the tournament hosts with three second half goals.
item.content:encoded:
New Zealand have qualified for a 13th FIFA U-17 World Cup and secured progression to the final of OFC U-16 Men's Championship 2026 with a 3-0 win over Papua New Guinea at the PNG Football Stadium in Port Moresby.

They move on to face Fiji in Saturday's title-decider at the same venue, overcoming a spirited display from the tournament hosts with three second half goals.

Papua New Guinea had the gloves of goalkeeper Luther Rodney to thank for keeping New Zealand frustrated through the first 45 minutes.

Twice he denied opposing captain Ben Trenberth, first from a one-one-position, and then from a header at the back post.

Both of New Zealand’s goals in their 2-0 win over Papua New Guinea at this same stage of the competition last year came in the second half, and this one followed a similar script.

The Junior All Whites let loose after the break.

Davi Valle Dos Santos provided the breakthrough in the 50th minute, squaring the ball to the back post, where Peitersen’s effort deflected off defender Bomecge Basanuc and into the bottom-left corner of the net.

Pietersen got his goal ten minutes later instead, meeting a cross into the box from Jack Clegg that he calmly tucked into the opposite corner.

It wasn't all one-way traffic though, and Will Martin's teammates were congratulating him when the New Zealand goalkeeper got up acrobatically to tip away a deflected shot from Papua New Guinea substitute Klivet Ronald.

Pietersen added the third goal in the dying moments of second half stoppage-time, getting in-behind the Papua New Guinea defence to round out a comfortable win for the Junior All Whites.

New Zealand will be chasing an 11th consecutive OFC U-16 Men's Championship title when they take to the field on Saturday, whilst Papua New Guinea will have one final chance to qualify for a first FIFA U-17 World Cup when they take on the Solomon Islands in the third place play-off.

New Zealand: 3 [Own Goal 50’, Cruz PIETERSEN 60’, 90+5') Papua New Guinea: 0

item.title: Parliament drives payroll reform
item.description: The transfer of the public service payroll system from the Department of Finance to the Department of Personnel Management [DPM] has been described as a major reform driven by parliamentary oversight following years of delays and serious weaknesses identified in an independent review. Chairman of the Parliamentary Committee on Public Sector Reform, Gary Juffa, said the launch shows that parliament
item.content:encoded:
The transfer of the public service payroll system from the Department of Finance to the Department of Personnel Management [DPM] has been described as a major reform driven by parliamentary oversight following years of delays and serious weaknesses identified in an independent review.

Chairman of the Parliamentary Committee on Public Sector Reform, Gary Juffa, said the launch shows that parliamentary committees can do more than simply table reports in Parliament.

"Today is testament to a Parliamentary Committee pushing its limits to drive accountability of public administration," Mr Juffa said.

"Public sector reform in this country must not merely be announced, but delivered. Not merely funded, but implemented. And not merely celebrated, but measured."

Mr Juffa acknowledged the Marape-Rosso Government for supporting parliamentary committees with the resources needed to carry out oversight work, even when those committees have been critical of the government.

He also reminded those attending that concerns over the public service payroll were first identified more than a decade ago.

Mr Juffa said the decision to conduct a payroll audit was originally made by the O'Neill Government in 2014, but it was not implemented until the Marape-Rosso Government took office in 2019 and ensured the review was carried out.

The independent Deloitte Payroll Diagnostic Review, completed in April 2023, uncovered 53 findings across the public service payroll system, with 27 classified as high-impact and high-priority, along with 135 recommendations for reform.

Among the major issues identified were deceased and terminated employees who remained on the payroll and continued receiving salaries, while some individual payments exceeding K100,000 in a single fortnight were processed without proper review.

Mr Juffa said Deloitte also identified a serious conflict of interest because the Department of Finance was responsible for using, administering and paying through the payroll system.

"The Department of Finance was, at one and the same time, a user of the payroll system, the administrator of the payroll system, and the payer of every public servant in this nation."

He said Deloitte concluded that the concentration of those responsibilities meant existing controls over the payroll process had "little or no effect at all."

The report recommended that payroll administration be returned to the Department of Personnel Management under the Public Service Management Act, with the establishment of a dedicated public service payroll office.

Mr Juffa noted that while the recommendation has now been implemented, it took three years and three months after Deloitte submitted its report.

"Reform delayed is money lost and it is trust lost."

He cautioned that simply transferring payroll responsibilities would not solve the problem unless strong internal controls, regular audits and proper segregation of duties are maintained within DPM.

"Moving the keys does not remove the risk, it moves it."

Mr Juffa said Parliament will continue monitoring the reform and expects DPM to report back within six months on progress made in removing ghost employees, cleaning payroll records, reviewing user access and upgrading the ALESCO payroll system.

item.title: Opposition calls for immediate swearing-in of new Electoral Commissioner
item.description: Opposition Leader James Nomane has urged the Prime Minister and the Chief Secretary to immediately ensure that Papua New Guinea's newly appointed Electoral Commissioner, Professor Nicole Haley, is sworn into office.

item.title: Nine-Mile eviction victims seek justice during Minister’s visit
item.description: Families evicted from Portion 11, 55 & 56 at 9 Mile have called for an investigation into the ownership of the land, saying they have lived on the property for more than four decades.
